message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

I am deduplicating password-protected 7z archives on my Synology DS223j NAS.
  Continue the workflow established in a prior session.

  CONNECTION
  - Primary NAS: ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.246  (key-based auth, no password)
  - Secondary NAS: ssh bhyslop@169.254.105.91 (direct-cabled to Mac en0, not in use
    for this dedup task; ignore unless I bring it up)

  LAYOUT
  - Home dir is a symlink: /var/services/homes/bhyslop/ -> /volume1/homes/bhyslop/
  - Source tree:  ~/my-stash-from-dan/{FROM_BLUE--1, FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--1,
    FROM_SILVER--1}/{from-corsair-240g, from-float1, from-float2, from-gauntlet,
    from-horde, from-usb-sd-misc}/*.7z
  - Canonical destination: ~/bhyslop-vault/  (flat directory, one copy per
    archive, original filenames preserved)

  WORKFLOW PER ARCHIVE
  1. sha256sum all copies on all drives that have a file with matching basename.
  2. If all hashes match: mv one copy to ~/bhyslop-vault/, rm the rest.
  3. If hashes differ (even with matching name + size): run `7z t <path>` on each
     candidate. It will prompt for a password; I will type it interactively. Keep
     whichever copy tests clean; rm the ones that fail. If multiple copies test
     clean but have different hashes, stop and ask me — they are independent
     legitimate archives, not dedup partners.
  4. Verify: vault has the file, all source paths empty, no strays elsewhere
     under ~.  Do this with stat/ls/find, NEVER by re-hashing (that wastes hours
     on big files).

  RULES
  - Never use `sudo` — no cached password, it will fail.
  - Never run `find` or `grep` via Bash tool — use Glob/Grep tools. For NAS-side
    filesystem inspection, go through ssh.
  - Do not write a batch script that loops over all files. I want
    one-triple-at-a-time with me in the loop, because archives are huge
    (11 GB–180 GB each) and sha256 / 7z t each take 5–60 min.
  - Process order: smallest candidate first. Use stat-based sizes to sort.
  KNOWN CAVEAT (critical — do not forget)
  - Synology Drive Server is enabled and is holding reflinked shadow copies of
    everything in homes/ under /volume1/@synologydrive/. Until Drive is
    uninstalled or its version history purged, `rm` in homes/ does NOT free
    actual disk space — btrfs refcounts stay >0. The dedup work is still
    correct (simplifies the tree and sets up the future free), but df will
    keep saying 98 % full until Drive is dealt with.
  - Do not propose running `duperemove`, reflink tricks, or anything that
    modifies the filesystem's extent layout. Keep it boring: sha256sum, mv, rm.

  ALREADY PROCESSED (in vault):
  - bhyslop-dazzler-w10-20230131-1.7z   (126 GiB, from triple)
  - 20080313-aarons-p2-13G-drive.float2-1.7z   (11.7 GiB, from triple)
  - 20230207-old-latitude-group.float2-1.7z   (11.5 GiB, BLUE+SILVER matched,
    SEAGATE was not a valid 7z archive and was deleted)

  OUT OF SCOPE (do not process unless I ask)
  - All from-float1/ files
  - All bhyslop-dazzler-w10-*.7z files (one more big triple remains; I will
    handle it later)
  - Any file with .broken, .broken1, .broken2, .REDO in its name (those are
    failed archive attempts or deliberate re-runs, not dedup partners)

  NEXT FILE TO PROCESS
  Target: 20230210-usb-sd-misc.1.7z   (17.3 GiB, 2-way pair: SEAGATE + SILVER
  only, matching size + mtime). Begin by showing me the sha256sum command you
  want me to run; I'll send the output.

  Remaining candidates after this one, smallest first (for context):
    17.9 GiB  20230210-Corsair-240g-select.1.7z        (3-way: BLUE+SEA+SIL)
    47.2 GiB  20080311-my-old-80G-drive.flloat2-1.7z   (3-way: BLUE+SEA+SIL)
    68.7 GiB  20230210-gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.REDO.7z  (2-way, 6-min mtime skew)
    68.7 GiB  20230210-gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.7z       (2-way, 7-day mtime skew ⚠)
    106.7 GiB 20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float2-1.7z   (3-way: BLUE+SEA+SIL)
    178.3 GiB 20230207-horde-black-seagate-usb.1.7z    (2-way: SEA+SIL)
    178.3 GiB 20230207-horde-black-seagate-usb.2.7z    (2-way: SEA+SIL)

  Verify you can reach the NAS by running `hostname` on it, then give me the
  sha256sum command for the usb-sd-misc pair.

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

```
ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.246 'sha256sum ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--1/from-usb-sd-misc/20230210-usb-sd-misc.1.7z & sha256sum ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SILVER--1/from-usb-sd-misc/20230210-usb-sd-misc.1.7z & wait'
```

Paste both lines of output when it finishes.

content: Deduplicating password-protected 7z archives on the Synology NAS into ~/bhyslop-vault. Current target is the 2-way pair 20230210-usb-sd-misc.1.7z on SEAGATE and SILVER. Next: you run the parallel sha256sum command I gave and paste both lines.

message.content: Can you give me a command to check the line rate of the .246 nas?  I wonder if it is 100 or 1000 bt

content.text:
The cleanest no-sudo, no-ethtool way is sysfs:

```
ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.246 'for i in /sys/class/net/eth*; do echo "$i: $(cat $i/speed 2>/dev/null) Mb/s, duplex=$(cat $i/duplex 2>/dev/null), carrier=$(cat $i/carrier 2>/dev/null)"; done'
```

Prints one line per ethX interface with speed in Mb/s (100 or 1000), duplex, and carrier. No package dependencies, no root.

message.content:
bhyslop@DS223j:~$ for i in /sys/class/net/eth*; do echo "$i: $(cat $i/speed 2>/dev/null) Mb/s, duplex=$(cat $i/duplex
>   2>/dev/null), carrier=$(cat $i/carrier 2>/dev/null)"; done
/sys/class/net/eth0: 1000 Mb/s, duplex=full, carrier=1
bhyslop@DS223j:~$

content.text: Gigabit full-duplex, link up. So ~110-115 MB/s is the practical ceiling — the 17.3 GiB hash should read-bound at around 2.5-3 minutes per copy if the drives cooperate (parallel, since they're separate spindles). Ready when you are.

content.text:
Hold on — there's a problem. Only two hashes ran, not three. Look at the pasted command:

```
sha256sum FROM_BLUE... &
sha256sum FROM_SEAGATE... & sha256sum
                                        ↑
                              got cut off — no third path
```

The third `sha256sum` on the SILVER copy never executed (you Ctrl-C'd the bare `sha256sum` waiting on stdin). So:

- BLUE hash: a70e27... ✓
- SEAGATE hash: a70e27... ✓
- **SILVER hash: never computed**

Then you moved SEAGATE to vault and deleted BLUE — but the SILVER copy is still sitting in `FROM_SILVER--1/from-corsair-240g/` and has never been verified against the vault copy. Let me confirm.

content.text:
Understood — that `7z t` on BLUE was evidently run *before* you `rm`'d it (the current state shows BLUE gone). It's a useful retroactive attestation that BLUE was a structurally valid archive, but it doesn't close the SILVER gap.

We're still in the same spot: **SILVER is the unverified copy.** We know the target hash (a70e275884337f982a9a954816459fe9f5aeac982232269656285cf8e1a56fd8) from BLUE and SEAGATE agreeing. One sha256sum on SILVER answers the question; no password prompt, fully non-interactive:

```
ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.246 'sha256sum ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SILVER--1/from-corsair-240g/20230210-Corsair-240g-select.1.7z'
```

- If it prints `a70e27...` → `rm` SILVER, done.
- If it prints anything else → stop, show me, we decide together.

(If you'd rather do `7z t` on SILVER instead, that also works — it's a structural attestation rather than a bit-for-bit match, and it's slower because of the password prompt and equivalent read cost. The hash is the cleaner close-out.)

Two notes before I verify:

1. **Scope**: your original brief flagged `bhyslop-dazzler-w10-*.7z` as out-of-scope ("one more big triple remains; I will handle it later"). You apparently decided to handle it now — just flagging in case that was inadvertent.
2. **The failed `7z t`**: three independent copies hashed to the same 746f82ac... — that's already stronger evidence than a single `7z t` pass. The wrong-password error on the post-mv test doesn't invalidate anything; the workflow only invokes `7z t` as a tiebreaker when hashes *disagree*. You can ignore it.

The `.broken2` file (different hash, different basename) is correctly untouched per the brief's exclusion rule.

Verifying state now.

content.text:
All four present. Size check:
- `.1.7z`: 191,405,911,342 on both drives ✓
- `.2.7z`: 191,405,912,158 on both drives ✓ (note: `.2` is 816 bytes larger than `.1` — a real difference, they're distinct archive parts, not duplicates of each other)
- Same Feb 8 2023 mtime across all four.

Run — this parallelizes by drive (one `sha256sum` per spindle, each hashing its two files serially, both spindles working concurrently):

```
ssh bhyslop@192.168.1.246 'sha256sum ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--1/from-horde/20230207-horde-black-seagate-usb.{1,2}.7z & sha256sum ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SILVER--1/from-horde/20230207-horde-black-seagate-usb.{1,2}.7z & wait'
```

Rough estimate at Corsair-era throughput: ~30 min per file, two files per drive, drives in parallel → ~60 min wall clock. Might be CPU-bound (sha256 on the DS223j's Realtek ARM), so parallelism helps less than you'd hope but doesn't hurt.

Paste all four hash lines when done.

content.text:
Grouping by hash, respecting scope rules (float1, .broken*, .REDO excluded):

## Ready: two clean 3-way matches

**`20080311-my-old-80G-drive.flloat2-1.7z`** (47.2 GiB) — all three drives match:
```
fb3add7a...  BLUE/from-float2
fb3add7a...  SEAGATE/from-float2
fb3add7a...  SILVER/from-float2
```

**`20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float2-1.7z`** (106.7 GiB) — all three drives match:
```
6743911c...  BLUE/from-float2
6743911c...  SEAGATE/from-float2
6743911c...  SILVER/from-float2
```

## Divergent: one confirmed-independent pair

**`20230210-gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.7z`** (68.7 GiB, in-scope):
```
ebb17ce0...  SEAGATE/from-gauntlet
372d3a8a...  SILVER/from-gauntlet   ← different
```

Your brief already flagged this pair ⚠ 7-day mtime skew; the hash mismatch confirms they're independent archives, not dedup partners. Per workflow: run `7z t` on each; if both test clean, keep both under different names. **Defer this one** — it needs a rename decision and a password. I'd handle it after the easy wins.

## Not acted on

- float1/ entries (out of scope per brief) — for the record, several happen to cross-match cleanly across BLUE+SEAGATE, but we aren't touching them.
- `.REDO` and `.broken1` entries — out of scope.
- The gauntlet `.REDO` pair actually matches (`6224ae7d...` on both drives), but `.REDO` is out of scope, so no action.

## Commands for the two ready ones

Proposed — keep the BLUE copy in each case (matches what you did for the dazzler-2 close-out):

```
# 47 GiB flloat2-1
mv ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_BLUE--1/from-float2/20080311-my-old-80G-drive.flloat2-1.7z ~/bhyslop-vault/
rm ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--1/from-float2/20080311-my-old-80G-drive.flloat2-1.7z
rm ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SILVER--1/from-float2/20080311-my-old-80G-drive.flloat2-1.7z

# 107 GiB float2-1
mv ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_BLUE--1/from-float2/20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float2-1.7z ~/bhyslop-vault/
rm ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--1/from-float2/20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float2-1.7z
rm ~/my-stash-from-dan/FROM_SILVER--1/from-float2/20080312-my-old-120G-drive.float2-1.7z
```

Run them; paste anything you want double-checked.
